text: The video shows regular adhesions in the anterior abdominal wall and left upper quadrant, related to previous laparotomy incisions, with visible omental adhesions.

The surgeon encounters increasingly dense and fibrotic adhesions as they progress towards the right iliopsoas region. The omentum is being carefully dissected from the anterior abdominal wall, revealing the complexity of the adhesions.
The surgical team works to achieve proper exposure of the right iliac fossa, preparing the area for further adhesiolysis.
The surgeon focuses on the small bowel adhesions and the area of the right iliac fossa, carefully listing and addressing the omental and upper abdominal adhesions visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection of dense adhesions using cold scissors, avoiding the use of monocular energy to prevent thermal injuries. Occasionally, a harmonic scalpel is used for vascularized adhesions.

The surgeon carefully dissects fibrotic bands between bowel loops and between the bowel loops and the parietal and retroperitoneal areas, aiming to free the small bowel and restore proper anatomical orientation. The surgeon inspects the cut sites to ensure there is no perforation or violation of the small bowel lumen.

The surgeon carefully releases the terminal ileum and cecum from the retroperitoneum, focusing on the area of the right iliac fossa. Attention is given to avoid injury to the nearby iliac vessels and ureter.

The surgeon methodically releases small bowel adhesions and phrenic adhesions, working incrementally from different areas of the abdominal cavity. This approach helps maintain orientation and perspective, allowing for better anatomical reconstruction and connection of the surgical areas.

The surgeon approaches the area of the previous appendectomy and the psoas muscle, encountering very thick, tough, bony-like adhesions. Using scissors, the surgeon carefully cuts through the dense fibrotic tissue, noting the hardness of the tissues upon touch.

The surgeon has completely taken down the omentum that was adhered to the iliac fossa, descending colon, and ileocecal junction, flipping it over to the upper abdomen. This maneuver allows for tracing the ascending colon both upwards and downwards to accurately localize the ileocecal junction, ensuring the small bowel is free up to this area.

The surgeon isolates two severely adhered bowel loops in the iliac fossa, characterized by hard, fibrotic, bony-like adhesions. The loops are further dissected to free them completely from the adhesions. The iliac vessels and psoas muscle are starting to be visualized in the surgical field.

The surgeon continues to dissect the adherent loops of bowel in the area of the right iliac fossa and the psoas muscle. The dissection aims to reach the pathological area and potentially free the small bowel completely from the adhesions.

The surgeon is dissecting around the area of the erythropoietin problem, revealing the ureter and iliac vessel. The bowel loops and mesentery have been separated from this area, ensuring the safety of the vessels and ureter. The surgeon passes between the mesentery of the bowel loop and the ureter, further dissecting to increase the mobility of the bowel loops away from the pathological site.

The surgeon reaches the posterior area of the psoas abscess, liberating the bowel adherent to it. The tissues appear shaggy and fibrotic, with whitish fibrotic adhesions visible in the area of the psoas muscle. The dissection continues to expose the psoas abscess and release the idiosyncratic junction from the dense adhesions.

The surgeon mobilizes the area around the ileocecal junction, releasing adhesions from below and directing attention to the dissection from above. The grasper holds an unclear structure on the left side, which will be identified later as the dissection planes converge.

The surgeon dissects the area of the psoas abscess, entering the abscess cavity where pus and a stone-like object are expelled. The wall of the abscess cavity within the psoas muscle is visible. The cecum is being released from the pathological area, and the cavity is being cleaned of necrotic debris and infected material.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to achieve hemostasis on necrotic tissue and the abscess wall after releasing the cecum from the area of the psoas abscess. The abscess, which opened posteriorly into the psoas muscle, is cleaned up, and the chronic pain area is delineated. The cecum and ascending colon are further released from the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon liberates the cecum and ascending colon from remaining attachments, flipping them upward to examine their posterior aspects. The cecum and ascending colon are now completely dislocated from the abscess area, revealing the stone, granulation tissue, and abscess cavity wall.
The surgeon excises a portion of the fibrotic tissue from the area surrounding the psoas abscess for pathological assessment, aiming to reduce the risk of future collection or recurrence.

The surgeon identifies the appendix, which was thought to have been removed 14 years ago, intact and wedged between the terminal ileum and the cecal wall. Dense fibrous tissue surrounds the appendix, which is likely the source of the abscess in the psoas muscle and chronic inflammation. The base of the appendix is visible, clarifying the cause of the patient's chronic pain and adhesions.

The apex of the appendix is engulfed in dense adhesions and fibrosis, intertwined with the mesentery of the ileum and part of the retroperitoneum. The surgeon cuts through the psoas muscle and abscess cavity surrounding the appendix tip, carefully separating these structures from the terminal ileum and its mesentery to remove the pathological tissue causing the patient's long-term suffering.

The surgeon replaces a 5mm port with a 12mm port to facilitate the removal of the appendix and pathological adhesions from the abscess cavity. Hemostasis is performed to ensure no bleeding occurs during the procedure.
